raw_text.response:
To calculate the orbital period of your spacecraft around the Sun, closely following Mercury's path, we can use **Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion**. This law relates the orbital period of a planet (or spacecraft) to its average distance from the Sun.

### Kepler's Third Law:
\[
T^2 = \left(\frac{4 \pi^2}{G M}\right) a^3
\]
Where:
- \( T \) = orbital period (in seconds)
- \( G \) = gravitational constant (\( 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{N} \, \text{m}^2 \, \text{kg}^{-2} \))
- \( M \) = mass of the Sun (\( 1.99 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg} \))
- \( a \) = average distance from the Sun (in meters)

### Given:
- Mercury's average distance from the Sun: \( a = 58 \times 10^6 \, \text{km} = 58 \times 10^9 \, \text{m} \)
- Mass of the Sun: \( M = 1.99 \times 10^{30} \, \text{kg} \)

### Step 1: Plug the values into Kepler's Third Law
\[
T^2 = \left(\frac{4 \pi^2}{6.674 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.99 \times 10^{30}}\right) (58 \times 10^9)^3
\]

### Step 2: Simplify the equation
First, calculate the term inside the parentheses:
\[
\frac{4 \pi^2}{6.674 \times 10^{-11} \times 1.99 \times 10^{30}} \approx 2.97 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{s}^2 \, \text{m}^{-3}
\]

Next, calculate \( a^3 \):
\[
(58 \times 10^9)^3 = 1.95 \times 10^{32} \, \text{m}^3
\]

Now, multiply the two results:
\[
T^2 = 2.97 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.95 \times 10^{32} \approx 5.79 \times 10^{13} \, \text{s}^2
\]

Take the square root to find \( T \):
\[
T = \sqrt{5.79 \times 10^{13}} \approx 7.61 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}
\]

### Step 3: Convert seconds to Earth days
\[
T = \frac{7.61 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}}{60 \times 60 \times 24} \approx 88 \, \text{Earth days}
\]

### Final Answer:
Your spacecraft will take approximately **88 Earth days** to complete one orbit around the Sun, closely following Mercury's path.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Imagine you're planning a space mission to Mercury and need to calculate how long your spacecraft will take to complete one orbit around the Sun, given Mercury's average distance from the Sun is 58 million kilometers.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: The mass of the Sun is 1.99x10^30 kg.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: How many Earth days will your spacecraft take to orbit the Sun once, closely following Mercury's path?

context
nodes.text:
\begin{theorem}
Let the gravitational constant be known.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Let the mean distance from the Earth to the sun be known.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Then it is possible to calculate the mass of the sun.
\end{theorem}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
\begin{proof}
From Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion:
:$T^2 = \left({\dfrac {4 \pi^2} {G M} }\right) a^3$
where:
:$T$ is the orbital period of the planet in question (in this case, the Earth)
:$a$ is the distance from the planet (in this case, the Earth) to the sun
:$M$ is the mass of the sun
:$G$ is the gravitational constant
In MKS units:
:$T = 60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365.24219 \, \mathrm s$ by definition of year
:$a = 149 \, 597 \, 870 \, 700 \, \mathrm m$ by definition of astronomical unit
:$G = 6.674 \times 10^{-11} \, \mathrm N \, \mathrm m^2 \, \mathrm{kg}^{-2}$ by measurement, as by hypothesis.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: :$M$ will be the result in kilograms.

nodes.label: context
context
nodes.text: The calculation has been left as an exercise for anyone who has the patience.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
{{qed}}
\end{proof}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
[Document 2]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Neptune]
'''Neptune''' is the eighth planet of the solar system from the sun.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Its orbit lies mainly between those of Uranus and Pluto, although the latter comes closer to the sun than '''Neptune''' on part of its orbit.
\end{definition}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
[Document 3]
\begin{definition}[Definition:New Moon]
The '''new moon''' is the astronomical phenomenon of the Moon in its orbit coming to its closest point to the Sun:
:800px

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Note that the above diagram is not to scale.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: At this point in its orbit, the Moon is between Earth and the Sun.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: Thus the hemisphere of the Moon which is illuminated by the Sun is facing directly away from Earth.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
Thus for an observer of the Moon from Earth, unless an actual solar eclipse is in progress, at this point it cannot be seen, because:
:$(1): \quad$ It is dark
:$(2): \quad$ The Sun is more or less behind it, outshining everything else in the sky.
Category:Definitions/Astronomy
\end{definition}

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
[Document 4]
\begin{definition}[Definition:Phase of Moon]
The '''phase of the Moon''' is its apparent shape when viewed in the night sky of Earth.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text:
This shape is dependent upon the position of the Moon with respect to the Sun and Earth.
:800px

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: When the Moon is between the Sun and Earth,

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the sunlit side faces more or less away from Earth, and so less than half of it can be seen lit up.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: When Earth is between the Sun and the Moon,

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the sunlit side faces more or less away towards Earth, and so more than half of it can be seen lit up.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: When the Moon is directly between the Sun and Earth,

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the sunlit side is facing completely away from Earth, and in normal conditions cannot be seen.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This is the '''phase''' known as '''new moon'''.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: When Earth is directly between the Sun and the Moon,

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: the sunlit side is facing completely towards Earth, and in normal conditions appears as a brign disk.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: This is the '''phase''' known as '''full moon'''.

nodes.label: context
nodes.text: At the point half way between new moon and full moon, the '''phase''' is known as '''first quarter'''.

nodes.text: Let the mean distance of Earth from $S$ be $A$.

nodes.text:
Thus the required orbital periods are:
:$\text{(a)}: \quad 2^{3/2} = 2 \sqrt 2 \approx 2.8$ years
:$\text{(b)}: \quad 3^{3/2} = 3 \sqrt 3 \approx 5.2$ years
:$\text{(c)}: \quad 25^{3/2} = 125$ years.

\end{proof}
